column1: https://doi.org/10.15454/SNRK0V‡https://doi.org/10.15454/SNRK0V‡https://doi.org/10.15454/SNRK0V‡inrae://10.15454/SNRK0V‡‡doi:10.15454/SNRK0V‡2021-07-01T13:51:09Z‡2021-06-28T06:33:19Z‡‡Dataset for the metabolic and physiological characterization of seeds from oilseed rape (Brassica napus L.) plants grown under single or combined effects of drought and clubroot pathogen Plasmodiophora brassicae‡Nesi
column3:
Grégoire‡"Lien vers les données
Faced with the challenges of adapting agriculture to climate change
column4: seed production should have increased resilience to abiotic stress factors and the expected proliferation of pathogens. This concerns both the nutritional quality and seed vigor
column5: two crucial factors in seedling establishment and yield. Both qualities are acquired during seed development
column6: but how environment influences the genetic and physiological determinisms of these qualities remains to be elucidated. With a world production of 71 Mt of seeds per year
column7: oilseed rape (Brassica napus) is the third largest oleaginous crop. But its productivity must cope with several abiotic stresses
column8: among which drought is one of the main constraints in current and future climate scenarios. In addition
column9: clubroot disease
column10: caused by the pathogen Plasmodiophora brassicae
column11: leads to severe yield losses for the Brassica crops worldwide. Clubroot provokes the formation of galls on the infected roots that can restrict the flow of water and nutrients within the plant throughout the growth cycle. In order to get new insights into the impact of single or combined constraints on seed qualities
column12: metabolic profiling assays were run for a collection of 330 seed samples (including developing
column13: mature and imbibed seeds) harvested from plants of two B. napus cultivars (“Express” and “Montego”) that were grown under either drought conditions
column15: or a combination of both stresses. Metabolites were identified and quantified by UPLC or GC. In addition
column16: monitoring germination traits was conducted for 60 mature seed lots under in vitro conditions using an automated phenotyping platform. The present dataset contains the raw contents for 42 metabolites (nmol.mg-1 of seed dry weight) filtered and analyzed with statistical tests as well as germination speed and percentages. This dataset is available under accession at Data INRAE. These data will contribute to a better understanding of the crosstalk between the plant responses to water deprivation and/or pathogen attack and how it compromises seed quality. A better understanding of the molecular and physiological responses of the seed to (a)biotic stress on a molecular and physiological will be a first step to meet scientific and technological challenges of adapting seeds to their environment."‡Oilseed rape

column2: insulin and dietary thresholds for prevention of exercise-associated hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia in free-living children with type 1 diabetes"""‡HEYMAN Elsa‡IQBAL
column3:
Asif‡"Lien vers les données
The datasets correspond to all data used in the analyses of the paper ""Explainable machine learning to identify activity-related
column4: insulin and dietary thresholds for prevention of exercise-associated hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia in free-living children with type 1 diabetes"". All information about the data collection are provided in the paper. Please find below a summary of the study and the results obtained with the data.  Physical activity induces immediate and delayed glycaemic changes in children with type 1 diabetes (T1D)
column5: yet real-life thresholds for exercise characteristics that jointly account for insulin and diet remain poorly defined. In a 7-day free-living study of 36 children with T1D
column6: we combined continuous glucose monitoring with self-reported and accelerometer-derived exercise measures
column7: alongside detailed insulin and dietary data. Ensemble machine-learning models predicted hypoglycaemia (<70 mg·dL⁻¹) and hyperglycaemia (>180 mg·dL⁻¹) during exercise
column8: the 2-hour post-exercise recovery
column9: and the subsequent night
column10: with thresholds identified using Shapley value analysis. The models achieved high predictive accuracy and revealed distinct phase-specific patterns. Pre-exercise glucose levels >175 mg·dL⁻¹ were associated with glucose decline during activity
column11: whereas levels >150 mg·dL⁻¹ predicted glucose increases during early recovery. Insulin boluses exceeding 11% of the total daily dose in the 4 h before exercise and 17% during the 2 h recovery period increased hypoglycaemia risk. Exercise effects were phase dependent: self-reported activity lasting >80 min increased hypoglycaemia risk during exercise
column12: while >15 min of objectively measured vigorous activity reduced risk during early recovery. A pre-sleep glucose range of 100–115 mg·dL⁻¹ minimised nocturnal dysglycaemia
column13: and behavioural patterns such as afternoon exercise and multiple daily sessions were associated with lower overnight hyperglycaemia. Patient characteristics
column17: influenced glycaemic risk in phase-specific and sometimes opposing ways. Overall
column19: phase-specific thresholds for exercise and insulin management across the exercise–recovery cycle
column20: moving beyond arbitrary categorisation used in previous studies."‡Child; Diabetes Mellitus

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/HZNS3S‡https://doi.org/10.57745/HZNS3S‡https://doi.org/10.57745/HZNS3S‡cnrs://10.57745/HZNS3S‡en‡doi:10.57745/HZNS3S‡2024-02-22T14:40:09Z‡2024-02-13T08:20:23Z‡‡Purchases emissions in research 1point5 (PER1p5) emission factors‡ESTEVEZ-TORRES
column3:
André‡"Lien vers les données
Monetary emission factors to estimate the carbon footprint associated to purchases in research laboratories. Emissions embodied in goods and services can be estimated by measuring physical or monetary flows. Physical estimates are more accurate if emission factors and inventories for each product exist. This is not the case for the sheer amount of goods and services purchased in research laboratories. To make the problem tractable
column4: goods were classified according to the French system for accountability in research (NACRES)
column5: to which we manually associated monetary emission factors (EFs) in kg CO2e/euro and for which monetary inventories are readily available through the laboratory accountability. All euro values correspond to year 2019. The emissions of good i can calculated as e(i) = p(i)* EF(i)
column6: with p(i) its tax-free price in euro. EFs were estimated using the three approaches sketched in Fig. 1 of the associated publication: i) an environmentally extended input-output (EEIO) method that we will call in the following macro and note the resulting EFs EFmacro; ii) a process-based method that we  call micro EFmicro (or LCA for life cycle assessment); and iii) an intermediate approach based on the carbon footprint of selected companies of the research sector
column7:
that we call meso. The detailed description of the files is in the tab README for each file and in the accompanying publication."‡Univ-Rennes; carbon footprint; emission factor; emission reduction; environnementally-extended input-output; procurement; research laboratory‡Dataset‡"Hceres_disciplines_final.csv

column3: Normandie
column4:
France‡Mouhamadoul Moustapha BA‡Mouhamadoul Moustapha BA‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset presents field measurements that documents the physical and chemical response of riverbeds in the context of critical hydrological and sedimentary forcing in the Selune River (France). The river flows into the bay of Mont Saint-Michel and thus impacts numerous economical activities and spawning of several key species such as Atlantic salmon and lamprey. To restore the hydro-sedimentary continuity of the river
column5: two dams are currently being removed. Important changes in the stream flow regime
column6: the stream-aquifer exchanges and the sediment transport are expected
column7: motivating the monitoring campaign.  A network of high frequency autonomous sensors (water level
column8: temperature
column9: conductivity
column10: oxygen and pressure differential) was installed in the river and the riverbed at various depths
column11: to continuously record the variation of the main physico-chemical variables and relate them surface processes. To assess the impact of dam removal on these variables
column12: two measurement sites were chosen
column13:
upstream of the dams where flow conditions remained stable and downstream of the dams where a large amount of fine sediment has been released. This original data can be used to uncover the biogeochemical functioning of the hyporheic zone and its coupling to dynamical flow and sedimentary processes."‡Univ-Rennes; barrage; oxygène dissous; perméabilité; rivière‡Dataset; Software‡"data_baro_upstream-virey.tab

column3:
Florence‡"Lien vers les données
Data were collected on 24 pigs that were video-monitored day and night under two contrasted conditions: thermoneutral (TN
column4: 22°C) and Heat Stress (32°C). All pigs were housed individually and had free access to an automatic electronic feeder delivering pellets four times a day
column5: and to water. After acquisition
column6: videos were processed using YOLOv11
column7: a real-time object detection algorithm object detector that uses a convolutional neural network (CNN)
column8: to extract the following behavioural traits: drinking
column9: willingness to eat
column10: lying down
column11: standing up
column12: moving around
column13: curiosity towards the littermate housed in the neighbouring pen
column14: and contact between the two animals (cuddling). A minute frequency basis was applied (each minute correspond to 150 frames processed) for a continuous period of 16 days
column15: spanning the two different thermal conditions (9 days on TN
column17: 1 day back to TN). The algorithm was first trained thanks to manual video analysis labelling at the individual scale. Consistency with the automatic electronic feeder’s data (also provided) was thoroughly checked. The dataset allows quantitative criterion to be analysed to decipher inter-individual differences in animal behaviour and their dynamic adaptation to heat stress. This dataset can be used to train any machine learning methods  for behaviour prediction from videos in conventional growing pigs."‡Univ-Rennes; behaviour; computer science; deep learning; pig; thermal stress; video recording‡Dataset; Image; Model‡‡etalab 2.0‡https://spdx.org/licenses/etalab-2.0.html‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Antoine‡Grissot

Data from manuscript ""Coordination of parental performance is breeding phase-dependent in the Dovekie (Alle alle)
column5: a pelagic Arctic seabird""
column6: published in PlosOne
column7:
doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0306796

Currently
column8: parental care is becoming increasingly perceived as male and female cooperation
column9: instead of being primarily shaped by sexual conflict. Most studies examining cooperating performance consider coordination of parental activities
column10: and doing so focuses on a short time-window including only one stage of breeding (i.e.
column11: incubation or chick rearing period). Here
column12: we considered the cooperation of breeding partners
column13: investigating the coordination of parental care in a long-lived seabird species with long and extensive biparental care
column16: and looked at the issue throughout the breeding season. To do so
column17: we collected in  a Dovekie colony in Svalbard (77◦00′ N
column19: some video recordings of Dovekie pairs during both stages of the breeding period
column20: that were transformed into behavioral data after video analysis. The present dataset contains the data resulting from the video analysis
column21: as well as the R script used to perform statistical analyses on these data
column22: and a summary of the results of the statistical analyses. For instance
column23:
we tested whether coordination was subject to small-scale changes within each stage and whether there was a relationship between coordination levels across the two stages. We found that the level of parental coordination is overall high and increases during the incubation period but decreases through the chick rearing phases. These results suggest that coordination is not a fixed behavior but breeding-phase dependent and provide insights into how parental care and parents’ cooperation is shaped by brood"‡Avifauna; Behavioral ecology; Feeding behavior; Life-history strategy; Marine environment; Univ-Rennes‡Dataset; Workflow‡"Data_Ms.RDS

column8: Chastanet G. and Bernot K. Optimized local synthetic conditions induce size reduction and phase purification in {[Fe(Htrz)<sub>2</sub>(trz)](BF<sub>4</sub>)}n spin crossover particles. Crystal Growth & Design 2023  vol.23
column10:
p.1076-1083. HAL : https://hal.science/hal-03945807

Dataset production context :
The well-known synthesis of the two polymorphs of the {[Fe(Htrz)2(trz)](BF4)}n spin crossover coordination polymer is explored with new template-free methods that allow a control over the local synthetic conditions. A “one-pot” synthesis approach is developed
column11: in which the solid reactants are mixed together before the addition of the solvent
column12: which is expected to generate instantaneous supersaturation conditions favoring the nucleation of particles over their growth. In a second method
column13: the addition of ultrasound pulses promotes the appearance of local “hot spots” that affect the local temperature and allow exploring a different region of the concentration–temperature phase diagram
column14: leading to an increase in the phase purity of the product. These two syntheses are compared to the classical method in which the reactants are first dissolved in separate solutions before being mixed. The use of a one-pot synthesis
column16: induces a downsizing of the particle size by a factor of 500 on their volume. The addition of ultrasound pulses allows moving from a mixture of polymorphs I and II of this compound to the pure phase I. These approaches open the way to more studies on the control over the size or phase purity in such molecular compounds
column17:
without the use of any surfactant.

column3: on carabid communities and weed seed predation in winter cereals‡CARBONNE
column4: Benjamin‡CARBONNE
column5:
Benjamin‡"Lien vers les données
This data repository is associated with the article ‘Cascading effects of landscape
column6: mediated by mesoclimate
column7: on carabid communities and weed seed predation in winter cereals’. It includes both the datasets used (climatic
column8: landscape
column9: agricultural practices and biological data). Below is the abstract of the article:   Agricultural intensification
column10: landscape simplification
column11: and climate change threaten biodiversity and ecosystem services in arable lands. Increasing semi-natural habitats and landscape heterogeneity can mitigate these impacts by providing diverse habitats
column12: resources
column13: and modifying mesoclimate. As effective natural enemies in arable lands
column14: carabids play a key role in pest and weed seed regulation and are influenced by field management and landscape. This study hypothesized that field management and landscape factors directly
column15: as well as indirectly (via mesoscale air temperature)
column16: influence carabid communities and weed seed predation. We sampled 77 winter cereal fields across 20 landscape windows selected along uncorrelated gradients of semi-natural habitat amount and landscape heterogeneity. We monitored air temperature
column18: and weed seed predation during two sampling periods in late spring and early summer 2023. Piecewise Structural Equation Models were built to test for the direct and indirect effects of local factors (farming practices and standing weeds)
column19: landscape and mesoclimate on carabids and weed seed predation. Results showed that the amount semi-natural habitats and landscape heterogeneity primarily influence carabid activity density and composition
column20: which in turn affect weed seed predation. Grasslands
column22: favour carabids but also increase maximum air temperature
column24: likely due to thermotolerance traits. In addition
column25: a cascading effect of farming practices on seed predation was also observed in late spring
column26: mediated by carabid activity-density. The study highlights the importance of semi-natural habitats
column28: and farming practices in shaping carabid communities and their ecosystem services in arable fields. We highlight the influence of the landscape components on mesoscale air temperatures
column29:
impacting the species composition of carabid communities and potentially affecting weed regulation services through seed predation."‡INRAE; CNRS; ECOBIO; IGEPP; Univ-Rennes‡Dataset; Other‡"ASCC.txt

column3:
Olivier‡"Lien vers les données
The Phaeoexplorer project sequenced 60 genomes corresponding to 44 brown algal and sister species. This dataset corresponds to supplementary information relating to the initial annotation of the Phaeoexplorer genomes and multiple analyses of the genome data. The dataset includes additional results of the project
column4: together with accompanying additional figures and tables
column5: (Additional_results.tar.gz)
column6: presubmission (v0) versions of the Phaeoexplorer genome annotation (GFF) files (GFF_v0.tar.gz) and genome-wide predicted proteomes as fasta files (Proteomes_v0.tar.gz)
column7: de novo transcriptome assemblies for the Phaeoexplorer species (RNA-seq data assembled with Trinity or rnaSPAdes; de-novo-transcriptomes.tar.gz)
column8: RepeatMasker analyses of repeat sequences (RepeatMasker.tar.gz)
column9: alignment files used to generate a phylogenetic tree for the Phaeoexplorer species (PhylogeneticTree.tar.gz)
column10: alignments used to build a densitree specifically for Ectocarpus species (Microevolution_Ectocarpus.tar.gz)
column11: an Orthofinder-based analysis of shared orthologues (Orthogroups.tar.gz) together with a Dollo-logic-based analysis of orthogroup gain and loss during evolution (Dollo_analysis.tar.gz)
column12: a Phylostratigraphy analysis of brown algal genes (Phylostratigraphy.tar.gz)
column13: an analysis of protein functional domain fissions and fusions (CompositeGenes.tar.gz)
column15: Hectar predictions of protein subcellular localisations (Hectar.tar.gz)
column16: eggNOG output providing information about predicted protein functions (eggNOG.tar.gz)
column17: RNA-seq-based data on gene expression levels (mRNAexpression.tar.gz)
column18: results of a search for genes acquired via horizontal gene transfer (HGT.tar.gz)
column19: analyses of intron conservation across genomes (Introns_conservation.tar.gz)
column20: an analysis of tandem gene duplications (Tandemely_duplicated_genes.tar.gz)
column21: CAFE5 reconstruction of gene family amplifications (CAFE5.tar.gz)
column22: comparisons of CDS size with the Ectocarpus reference genome that were used to evaluate gene model completeness (CDS_size.tar.gz)
column23: a DESeq2 analysis of differential gene expression between the sporophyte and gametophyte generations of several brown algal species (DEG_LifeCycle.tar.gz)
column24:
information about orthogroups selected to analyse the effects of morphological complexity and life cycle structure on gene evolution (Genes_selection.tar.gz). Each individual dataset contains a README file explaining its content. Detailed information about the methodology used for each analysis can be found in the Methods section of the manuscript preprint (https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.02.19.579948). "‡Data from the paper entitled 'Glycaemic effects of a 156-km ultra-trail race in athletes: an observational field study'‡Heyman
column2:
Elsa‡Cassandra Parent‡"Lien vers les données
Objectives
We aimed to explore the impact of an ultra-endurance race on continuously measured glycaemia and to understand potential physiological mechanisms
column3:
as well as the consequences for performance and behavioural alertness.
Methods
Fifty-five athletes (78% men
column4: 43.7 +/- 9.6 years) ran a 156-km ultra-trail race (‘Trail Scientifique de Clécy’: six 26-km laps
column5: total elevation +6000 m). Participants wore a masked continuous glucose monitoring sensor from the day before the race until 10 days post-race. Blood was taken at rest
column6: during refuelling stops after each lap
column7: and after 24-h recovery. Running intensity (% heart rate reserve)
column8: performance (lap times)
column9:
psychological stress and behavioural alertness were explored. Linear mixed models and logistic regressions were carried out.
Results
No higher risk of hypo or hyperglycaemia was observed during the exercise phases of the race (i.e.
column10: excluding stops for scientific measurements and refuelling) compared with resting values. Laps comprising a greater proportion of time spent at maximal aerobic intensity were nevertheless associated with more time >180 mg/dL (P=0.021). A major risk of hyperglycaemia appeared during the 48-h post-race period compared with pre-race (P<0.05)
column11: with 31.9% of the participants spending time >180 mg/dL during recovery vs. 5.5% during resting. Changes in circulating insulin
column12: cortisol and free fatty acids followed profiles comparable with those usually observed during traditional aerobic exercise. However
column14: and to a lesser extent lactate dehydrogenase
column15:
increased exponentially during the race (P<0.001) and remained high at 24-h post-race (P<0.001; respectively 43.6 and 1.8 times higher vs. resting). Glycaemic metrics did not influence physical performance nor behavioural alertness.
Conclusion
Ultra-endurance athletes were exposed to hyperglycaemia during the 48-h post-race period
column16:
possibly linked to muscle damage and inflammation. Strategies to mitigate muscle damage or subsequent inflammation before or after ultra-trail races could limit recovery hyperglycaemia and hence its related adverse health consequences."‡Glycaemia; Type 1 diabetes; Ultra-trail; Univ-Rennes‡Dataset‡"Behavioral alertness .tab

column4: 2018) and the outlet of Strengbach catchment in the “Observatoire Hydro-Geochimique de l’Environnement” (Pierret et al.
column5: 2018). Major ion concentrations have been measured using experimental prototypes of field laboratory called “Riverlabs” (Floury et al.
column6: 2017; Brekenfeld et al.
column7: submitted). The filtered water was analysed by a Dionex ICS-5000 Ion Chromatography system (Thermo Scientific) for the major cations (Na+
column8: Mg2+
column9: K+
column10: Ca2+) and anions (Cl-
column11: NO3-
column12: SO42) every 35 to 45 minutes at Kervidy-Naizin and every 20 to 30 minutes at Strengbach. Stream discharge at both sites was estimated at the catchment outlets every minute (Kervidy-Naizin) and every 2 minutes (Strengbach) based on long-term
column13: well-established rating curves and continuous water level monitoring.  The data sets includes all data used in the analysis published in Brekenfeld et al. (2024) which correspond to 62 rainfall-discharge events identified based on discharge variations and for which all concentrations data have been validated (no technical issue
column14:
no maintenance operation during the events) between June 2018 and August 2022. Details related to the monitoring protocol are provided in Brekenfeld et al. column5: populated with virtual humans in a spatially constrained environment (metro). It contains the file “MetroUE5.exe” which features the experiment and a file “README.txt” with instructions on how to run it.   The second folder “RawData”

This dataset contains the calibrated data of Distributed Temperature Sensing (DTS) measurements conducted at Signy in November 2017
column4: April 2018
column5: September 2018 and September 2019. In this study
column6: we investigate the potential of Active-Distributed Temperature Sensing (DTS) methods for measuring and mapping hyporheic flow in a lowland stream
column7: by burying a few hundred meters of heatable Fiber-Optic (FO) cables within streambed sediments. Active-DTS method can provide high-resolution measurements of saturated flow. Active-DTS methods rely on heating of a FO cable while monitoring the resulting temperature rise at high spatial and temporal resolution. When the method is applied in saturated porous media
column8: the temperature increase
column9: resulting from the artificial heat injection
column10: dependent on the water flux. Conducting Active-DTS in Signy showed that hyporheic fluxes vary by an one order of magnitude. The absence of correlation between water and streambed topography variations suggests that the spatiotemporal variability of fluxes may be used as a marker of the variability of streambed hydraulic conductivities. Here
column11:
the relatively low spatial variability in hyporheic flow suggests a small variability of streambed properties."‡Univ-Rennes; groundwater; heat conductivity; hyporheic zone; stream bed; surface water; temperature measurement‡Dataset‡"Apr18_Heated_FO1_1804_2004.xlsx
